cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A network of overland and sea trade routes connecting China, Central Asia, the Middle East, and the Mediterranean; major conduit for silk, porcelain, spices, and cultural exchange

cards.prompt: A roadside inn along major trade routes providing lodging, food, and water for merchants and their animals; facilitated long-distance overland trade by making multi-week journeys survivable

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The period of relative peace and stability across Central Eurasia under Mongol rule (c. 1270–1350) that facilitated unprecedented overland trade, communication, and cultural exchange

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The bubonic, pneumonic, and septicemic disease caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis that killed roughly one-third of Europe's population in the 14th century; spread along trade networks from Central Asia

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The Mongol khanate ruling China (1271–1368) founded by Kublai Khan; facilitated Silk Roads trade and allowed Marco Polo and other foreign merchants access to China

cards.prompt: The Mongol khanate ruling Persia and the Middle East (1256–1335); initially destroyed Baghdad but eventually converted to Islam and patronized Persian culture

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The Mongol khanate ruling Russia and Eastern Europe; extracted tribute from Russian principalities for two centuries, delaying Russian political development

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A seasonal wind pattern in the Indian Ocean basin that reverses direction twice yearly; enabled predictable sailing between East Africa, Arabia, India, and Southeast Asia

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The maritime trade network spanning from East Africa to Southeast Asia and China; the primary conduit for spices, textiles, and porcelain trade in 1200–1450

cards.prompt: Trade routes across the Sahara Desert linking West African gold and salt resources to Mediterranean markets; sustained by camel caravans and caravanserais

cards.prompt: A group of East African city-states on the Indian Ocean coast (Kilwa, Mombasa, Malindi) that became wealthy as intermediaries in the Indian Ocean trade network; developed a distinctive blend of African and Arabic culture

cards.prompt: A triangular-sailed ship used by Arab merchants in the Indian Ocean; maneuverable with lateen sails that allowed sailing against the wind

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A large Chinese sailing vessel with multiple compartments, bulkhead construction, and a stern-post rudder; used in Indian Ocean and South China Sea trade

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A device using a magnetized needle pointing north to determine direction; originally developed in China and adopted by sailors to navigate without landmarks

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A vertical steering mechanism attached to a ship's stern, replacing side-oars; improved maneuverability and made larger ocean-going vessels practical

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A trade center in present-day Mali that became the intellectual and commercial capital of the Mali Empire; home to the famous university mosque and thousands of Islamic scholars

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A Central Asian city on the Silk Roads that served as a major trading hub between China, Persia, and the Mediterranean world; famous for its bazaars and Islamic architecture

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A Central Asian oasis city at the eastern end of the Silk Roads; a major trading post on the route between China and Central Asia

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Explosive chemical composition originally developed in Tang Dynasty China; transferred to the Islamic world and then to Europe through trade networks, transforming medieval warfare

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Paper currency originally developed in Song Dynasty China; facilitated long-distance commerce by reducing the weight burden of metal coins

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A community of traders from one homeland who settled in a foreign city, maintaining cultural practices and commercial networks linking their new home to their place of origin

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A group of pack animals (typically camels) and merchants traveling together across deserts or long overland routes for safety and mutual support

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A modified saddle design that allowed a rider to sit more stably on a camel's back and carry heavier loads; made trans-Saharan trade commercially viable by enabling reliable long-distance camel caravans

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The title for a Mongol ruler; the supreme leader of a Mongol confederation or khanate

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The island archipelago in Southeast Asia (present-day Maluku Islands/Indonesia) that were the world's primary source of cloves, nutmeg, and mace; the ultimate destination of the Indian Ocean spice trade

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: High-fired ceramic ware developed in Tang and Song Dynasty China; a luxury trade good exported across the Indian Ocean world and eventually imitated in Islamic and European kilns

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: This 14th-century Moroccan Muslim scholar traveled over 75,000 miles across Afro-Eurasia, visiting courts from Mali to China and documenting trade networks, legal systems, and societies across the Islamic world

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: This 13th-century Venetian merchant traveled to Yuan Dynasty China and served Kublai Khan; his account introduced European audiences to the wealth and sophistication of East Asian civilization

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: This Chinese Muslim admiral led seven massive naval expeditions (1405–1433) across the Indian Ocean, visiting over 30 countries and demonstrating Ming China's maritime power through gift diplomacy

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: This 14th-century Mali emperor performed a hajj to Mecca in 1324–1325 with reportedly 60,000 people and so much gold that he caused inflation in Egypt; his pilgrimage put sub-Saharan Africa on European maps

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A series of military campaigns (1206–1368) in which nomadic warriors from Mongolia conquered the largest contiguous land empire in history, from China to Eastern Europe; initially devastating but ultimately created conditions for Eurasian trade and cultural exchange

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Beginning in the 1340s, this disease devastated Eurasia along trade routes, killing approximately one-third of Europe's population and reducing populations from China to Egypt; demonstrated how global trade networks could transmit catastrophic pathogens

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Seven voyages between 1405 and 1433 in which massive Chinese treasure ships visited ports from Southeast Asia to East Africa; these voyages were eventually discontinued by the Ming court, demonstrating that states could choose to withdraw from global exchange

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A merchant explains to a colleague in 1290 CE: 'Since the Mongols control both ends of the Silk Road, I can now travel from Hangzhou to Venice with a single safe-conduct pass.' Which of the following historical conditions best explains the merchant's confidence?

choices.text: The Pax Mongolica, in which Mongol political unification reduced banditry and provided consistent legal protections for merchants across Eurasia
choices.explanation: The Pax Mongolica refers to the relative peace and security Mongol rule provided across Central Eurasia — the merchant's ability to use a single pass reflects the unified legal framework that made long-distance overland trade safer and more commercially predictable.

choices.text: The Islamic Caliphate's restoration of Baghdad, which had rebuilt all trade infrastructure destroyed in the 1258 sack
choices.explanation: Baghdad was not rebuilt as an Islamic Caliphate seat under Mongol rule — the Il-Khanate governed the region and Baghdad never recovered its former status as the caliphate's capital in this period.

choices.text: Chinese military expansion that had established Song Dynasty garrisons along the Silk Roads to protect merchants
choices.explanation: The Song Dynasty was replaced by the Yuan (Mongol) Dynasty in 1279 — there were no Song garrisons along the Silk Roads in 1290. It was Mongol, not Chinese imperial, rule that provided security.

choices.text: The establishment of Christian Crusader kingdoms across the Middle East that guaranteed safe passage for all merchants
choices.explanation: By 1290 the Crusader presence in the Middle East was nearly over — Acre, the last major Crusader city, fell in 1291. Crusaders did not control the Silk Roads or offer universal safe conduct.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why the Swahili city-states of East Africa became prosperous centers of Indian Ocean trade between 1200 and 1450?

choices.text: Their location gave them access to African gold and ivory to export while providing a resupply point for monsoon-driven trade between Arabia and India
choices.explanation: Swahili cities like Kilwa occupied a strategic geographic position — they connected interior African gold and ivory to maritime networks while their natural harbors made them essential stopovers for Arab and Indian merchants sailing the monsoon routes.

choices.text: They conquered the Arabian Peninsula and used military force to redirect trade away from the Persian Gulf
choices.explanation: Swahili cities never conquered Arabia — they participated in Indian Ocean trade through commerce and diplomacy, not military conquest of their trading partners.

choices.text: They invented the monsoon-prediction technology that first made Indian Ocean sailing possible
choices.explanation: Monsoon patterns were known to Arab, Indian, and other sailors long before the Swahili city-states' rise — they benefited from existing monsoon trade routes rather than inventing monsoon navigation.

choices.text: They converted to Buddhism, forming a religious network with East Asian traders that excluded Muslim competition
choices.explanation: The Swahili city-states converted to Islam, not Buddhism — their Islamic identity connected them to Arab merchant networks and the broader Islamic commercial world of the Indian Ocean.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A historian argues that despite their destructive military conquests, the Mongols ultimately promoted long-distance trade more than any previous empire. Which of the following best supports this argument?

choices.text: The Mongol Empire unified much of Eurasia under a single legal framework, standardized weights and measures, and provided military protection for merchant caravans across the Silk Roads
choices.explanation: The Pax Mongolica's key contribution was creating a unified legal and security environment — when the same empire controlled both ends of the Silk Roads, political fragmentation no longer disrupted trade, enabling unprecedented commercial connectivity.

choices.text: The Mongols converted to Islam before their conquests and used religious networks to facilitate Muslim merchant trade
choices.explanation: Mongols did not convert to Islam before their conquests — some Il-Khanate Mongols converted later. The initial Mongol conquests were non-religious, and trade promotion preceded religious conversion.

choices.text: The Mongols destroyed all existing trade routes, forcing merchants to find new paths that opened more territories to commerce
choices.explanation: The Mongols largely maintained and improved existing trade routes rather than destroying them — the Silk Roads continued along established paths under Mongol protection.

choices.text: Mongol populations provided new markets for luxury goods, creating demand that stimulated production throughout Eurasia
choices.explanation: While the Mongol courts did consume luxury goods, this was a minor factor compared to the institutional changes — unified political control and security across trade routes was the primary driver of commercial expansion.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The decision by the Ming court to discontinue Zheng He's voyages after 1433 contrasted sharply with contemporary European states that were expanding their maritime exploration. Which of the following best explains why the Ming court chose to withdraw from overseas expansion?

choices.text: Neo-Confucian officials viewed maritime trade as beneath the dignity of a civilized empire and persuaded the emperor that resources were better spent defending against northern nomadic threats
choices.explanation: The Ming withdrawal from maritime exploration reflected a combination of Confucian ideological preferences for self-sufficient agrarian order over commerce, and practical military priorities — northern nomadic threats (the Mongols and later the Manchus) consumed military resources that the voyages were competing against.

choices.text: Portuguese naval technology had become so superior that the Chinese could no longer compete in Indian Ocean trade
choices.explanation: The Portuguese did not reach the Indian Ocean until 1498 — Zheng He's voyages ended in 1433, 65 years before the Portuguese arrived. Portuguese competition cannot explain the Ming withdrawal.

choices.text: The bubonic plague had eliminated the population needed to crew the treasure ships
choices.explanation: China's population had largely recovered from the 14th-century plague by 1433 — the crews for treasure ships were not a limiting factor in the Ming withdrawal decision.

choices.text: The Spice Islands refused to trade with Chinese ships, making the voyages commercially unprofitable
choices.explanation: Zheng He's voyages were politically motivated tribute expeditions, not primarily commercial ventures — the Spice Island merchants actually welcomed trade, and commercial unprofitability was not the stated reason for the withdrawal.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: In what way did the trans-Saharan trade routes and the Indian Ocean trade routes MOST fundamentally differ from each other in 1200–1450?

choices.text: Trans-Saharan trade was primarily overland using camels, while Indian Ocean trade relied on maritime sailing using seasonal winds
choices.explanation: The technological foundations of the two networks were fundamentally different — camel caravans crossing desert required different infrastructure (caravanserais, water sources) than monsoon-driven maritime trade (dhow and junk construction, port facilities).

choices.text: Trans-Saharan trade exclusively involved Islamic merchants while Indian Ocean trade was monopolized by Chinese merchants
choices.explanation: Both networks involved diverse merchant communities — Arab, Berber, and sub-Saharan African merchants participated in trans-Saharan trade, while Indian Ocean trade included Arab, Indian, Malay, Swahili, and Chinese merchants.

choices.text: Indian Ocean trade carried only luxury goods while trans-Saharan trade was primarily bulk agricultural commodities
choices.explanation: Both networks carried a mix of luxury and utilitarian goods — salt and gold (trans-Saharan) and spices, textiles, and porcelain (Indian Ocean) were all traded.

choices.text: Trans-Saharan trade operated without state support while Indian Ocean trade was entirely controlled by governments
choices.explanation: Both networks involved a mix of private merchants and state involvement — West African rulers controlled gold sources, and some Indian Ocean ports were state-managed, while private merchants dominated both systems.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A scholar observes that the spread of Islam to Southeast Asia in the 1200s–1400s followed Indian Ocean trade routes rather than military conquest. Which of the following best supports this observation?

choices.text: The first Muslim communities in Southeast Asia were concentrated in port cities, not inland agricultural regions, suggesting merchants brought Islam along trade networks
choices.explanation: The geographic distribution of early Southeast Asian Islam — concentrated in coastal trading ports like Malacca rather than inland kingdoms — directly supports the trade-route rather than conquest model of Islamic spread.

choices.text: Arab armies conquered Java and Sumatra in 1200 CE, establishing sultanates that forced conversion of the local population
choices.explanation: No Arab military conquest of Java or Sumatra occurred — Southeast Asian Islam spread through merchant contact and peaceful conversion, not military campaigns from Arabia.

choices.text: Buddhist monks from India converted to Islam and then spread the new faith using existing Buddhist monastery networks
choices.explanation: There is no historical record of Buddhist monks converting to Islam and spreading it through monastic networks — Islam spread through merchant contact and Sufi missionaries, not converted Buddhist institutions.

choices.text: Islamic missionaries received funding from the Chinese government to promote Islam as a counterweight to Hindu influences
choices.explanation: The Ming Chinese government promoted Chinese cultural practices, not Islam — there is no evidence of state-sponsored Chinese funding for Islamic missionary work in Southeast Asia.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A 14th-century North African Muslim intellectual whose major work the Muqaddimah introduced concepts of historical sociology and proposed that civilizations rise and fall in cycles

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A system where a large merchant deposits goods or currency with a trusted agent in a distant city, avoiding the need to personally travel with valuable commodities; reduced risk and enabled long-distance Islamic trade

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A commercial partnership common in the Islamic world in which one party provided capital and another provided labor or expertise, with profits divided by contract; facilitated long-distance trade by pooling resources
